en.extract:
The white-crowned wheatear, or white-crowned black wheatear (Oenanthe leucopyga) is a wheatear, a small passerine bird that was formerly classed as a member of the thrush family Turdidae, but is now more generally considered to be an Old World flycatcher, Muscicapidae.


Description
This large 17–18 cm long wheatear breeds in stony deserts from the Sahara and Arabia across to Iraq. It is largely resident but has occurred as a rare vagrant to western Europe as far away as Great Britain. It nests in crevices in rocks or walls laying 3-5 eggs.
Although most wheatear species have distinctive male and female plumages, adults of both sexes of this species are black except for a white rump and mainly white tail. Full adults have a white crown, not shown by young birds.
The similar black wheatear, Oenanthe leucura, also breeds in the western part of the white-crowned wheatear's range, but the latter has a black inverted T on its white tail, whereas white-crowned has only a black centre to its tail. The black wheatear never has a white crown.
This species mainly eats insects. This wheatear has a loud song, more varied than most of its relatives and often including mimicry.


Etymology
The genus name Oenanthe is derived from the Ancient Greek oenos (οίνος) "wine" and anthos (ανθός) "flower".  It refers to the northern wheatear's return to Greece in the spring just as the grapevines blossom.  The specific leucopyga is Ancient Greek from leukos, "white", and puge, "rump". "Wheatear" is not derived from "wheat" or any sense of "ear", but is a 16th-century linguistic corruption of "white" and "arse", referring to the prominent white rump found in many species.

[[Kebili]], Tunisia | status = LC | status_system = IUCN3.1 | status_ref = {{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2016 |title=''Oenanthe leucopyga'' |volume=2016 |article-number=e.T22710238A94240236 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T22710238A94240236.en |access-date=13 November 2021}} | taxon = Oenanthe leucopyga | authority = ([[Christian Ludwig Brehm|Brehm]], 1855) }} The '''white-crowned wheatear''', or '''white-crowned black wheatear''' ('''''Oenanthe leucopyga''''') is a [[wheatear]], a small [[passerine]] [[bird]] that was formerly classed as a member of the [[thrush (bird)|thrush]] family Turdidae, but is now more generally considered to be an [[Old World flycatcher]], Muscicapidae. == Description == This large 17–18 cm long wheatear breeds in stony deserts from the [[Sahara]] and [[Arabian Peninsula|Arabia]] across to [[Iraq]]. It is largely resident but has occurred as a rare vagrant to western Europe as far away as [[Great Britain]]. It nests in crevices in rocks or walls laying 3-5 eggs. Although most wheatear species have distinctive male and female plumages, adults of both sexes of this species are black except for a white rump and mainly white tail. Full adults have a white crown, not shown by young birds. The similar [[black wheatear]], ''Oenanthe leucura'', also breeds in the western part of the white-crowned wheatear's range, but the latter has a black inverted '''T''' on its white tail, whereas white-crowned has only a black centre to its tail. The black wheatear never has a white crown. This species mainly eats insects. This wheatear has a loud song, more varied than most of its relatives and often including mimicry. White-crowned wheatear (15787696376).jpg|Adult ''O. l. ernesti'', Israel ==Etymology== The genus name ''Oenanthe'' is derived from the [[Ancient Greek]] ''oenos'' (οίνος) "wine" and ''anthos'' (ανθός) "flower". It refers to the [[northern wheatear]]'s return to Greece in the spring just as the [[Vitis|grapevines]] blossom. The specific ''leucopyga'' is [[Ancient Greek]] from ''leukos'', "white", and ''puge'', "rump".{{cite book | last= Jobling | first= James A. | year= 2010| title= The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url= https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling | publisher=Christopher Helm | location = London, United Kingdom | isbn = 978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages =[https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling/page/n225 225], 280 }} "Wheatear" is not derived from "[[wheat]]" or any sense of "[[ear]]", but is a 16th-century [[linguistic corruption]] of "white" and "[[Buttocks|arse]]", referring to the prominent white rump found in many species.{{Cite OED |Wheatear }} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q912081}} [[Category:Wheatears|white-crowned wheatear]] [[Category:Birds of North Africa]] [[Category:Birds of the Middle East]] [[Category:Birds described in 1855|white-crowned wheatear]] [[Category:Taxa named by Christian Ludwig Brehm|white-crowned wheatear]]
